description: A traveller setting out or a child being welcomed may receive 'thorai': words or a ritual act that offer goodwill towards a person, place, undertaking, or living thing. 'pula' (wish) presents a desired outcome, while a blessing directs that wish towards a recipient. The blessing is complete when it is offered; any later protection or good fortune remains its own event.
articulatory_notes: Rest the tongue tip lightly against the upper teeth for unvoiced 'th' and pass air into middle-height back rounded 'o'. Tap or briefly trill the tongue at the alveolar ridge before stressed open central 'a'. Keep final high-front unrounded 'i' in its own syllable across the 'a.i' hiatus, raising the tongue without inserting a glide.
sound_symbolism: The unvoiced dental opening makes the first breath audible, and the tap gives a brief pulse before the final hiatus opens into high 'i'. That outward unfolding can help a learner remember a blessing spoken towards another.
usage_notes: The recipient of the blessing precedes 'thorai'. A ceremony, departure, meal, or other occasion may appear in a nearby phrase. Noun 'thorai' refers to the blessing itself.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: A metta practice may use a blessing to direct goodwill towards a particular being. The practice lies in offering that goodwill; any change in either person must be observed afterwards.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Spoken blessings often accompany departures, planting, and life transitions in oral traditions. 'thorai' keeps the act available while an inherited formula remains with its own source.

semantic_domains.communication: goodwill addressed to a recipient through words or another expressive act
semantic_domains.community: a blessing offered within a relationship or at a shared transition
semantic_domains.ritual: a formal or repeated act that directs goodwill towards its recipient
